story:
Thwack! A snowball clips my shoulder and jolts me forward. I clutch the grocery bag, my knit cap catapulting into a snowbank. With a mittened thumb I push up my glasses and scan the quiet street like a detective with fogged lenses. The sun breaks through hazy clouds; the glare makes my teeth pound out Beethoven’s Fifth. Cap retrieved, I hustle along the slippery sidewalk on booted heels, trying not to face-plant.

This trip home has been one big pain. I maxed my credit card for the ticket to Boston, and apparently someone painted a bull’s-eye on my back. Worst of all, I turn thirty tomorrow—and Brad didn’t come with me.

Just as I step onto the shoveled driveway, my father’s cooling car goes ping-ping-ping. Something moves in my periphery. Rusty, our family dog, launches. His paws thump my coat and down I go, flat into a pile of snow. His tongue turns my face into a salt lick. “Yuck! Off, you mangy mutt!” I wheeze, then put my nose to his snout. “Can’t you spot a cat lover?” He knows I adore him. One last swipe and he bounds away, tail flagging.

I can’t outrun thoughts of Brad. He’d looked so hopeful when he told me his ex, Julie, called. She walked out on him and their son, Tommy. How could he still have feelings for her? Mom warned me he might need time to shake the past—and that jealousy would shove him right back at Julie. But how am I supposed to be Zen when he’s spending my birthday weekend with her?

Zing! Another snowball whistles past and explodes on the porch rail. I sprint up the stairs at the risk of my dignity and manage to toss over my shoulder, “Ha! You missed!”

Heat and light inside turn me briefly blind. Dad peeks over his newspaper. “Hi, Pumpkin. Did you see me drive by?”

“No. I’ve been… preoccupied.”

“Understatement. You okay?”

Guilt makes me nod and kiss his cheek. In the kitchen, cinnamon floats in the air like a spell. Mom is on her cell: “So it’s where I suggested, and you’re keeping an eye on it? Perfect!” She startles when I say hello.

“Who’s keeping an eye on what?” I ask.

“Peggy. Next door. She’s dog-sitting,” Mom chirps, slicing apples as if that’s always been true.

She glances up. “About Brad’s ‘hopeful’ look—what if it was about Tommy? Maybe he wants Julie to re-enter their son’s life.”

Even the whisper of that possibility makes my heart do a shaky pirouette.

“You said he gave you a birthday present?”

I pull a tiny, feather-light box from my pocket. “Feels empty.” I promised Brad I’d wait till midnight. Mom grins. “Oh, go on.”

I tear the paper. Inside: a note. “You’ll find your gift on the nose of a frosty old man.”

“Snowman,” Mom says, all innocence, eyes sparkling.

I yank on my jacket and slide outside. Rusty barrels toward me; I sidestep and he sails into a drift, pops back out, and shakes himself into a storm. I circle the snowman’s ample backside—and stop.

Balanced on the carrot nose glitters the most exquisite diamond ring I’ve ever seen.

My parents, framed in the doorway, are beaming.

Smack! A snowball thuds into my backside. I spin. Brad steps from behind the tree, sheepish grin, cheeks red, thumb up to my co-conspirators. The truth I’ve been pocketing all day flares bright: I’m the one he wants—past and ex and not-so-narrow hips and all.

I fly into his arms, kiss him breathless. He laughs, pulls me close, and whispers against my ear, “I take it that’s a yes?”
